pattern: One bomb's blast reaching another doesn't mean the reverse is true, so the bombs form a directed reachability graph, not an undirected one, and the answer is simply the largest reachable set found by starting a search from each bomb in turn.
hints: A bomb with a bigger radius can trigger a smaller one without the smaller one being able to trigger it back -- draw a directed edge from bomb i to bomb j whenever j's location falls within i's blast radius.
Comparing distances with square roots invites floating-point trouble -- compare squared distance against squared radius instead, which stays exact in integer arithmetic.
Since there are at most 100 bombs, you can afford to run a full traversal (BFS or DFS) from every single bomb as the detonation start and just keep the largest count of bombs reached.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, detonating the smaller-radius bomb does not reach the other, but the reverse does
statement example, two bombs too far apart to reach each other either way
statement example, a chain reaction cascades through all five bombs
single bomb, trivially detonates just itself
two bombs at the exact same location, radius always reaches distance zero
a large bomb reaches everything but nothing reaches back to it, so starting elsewhere gives a worse answer
three bombs in a one-directional cycle-free chain where only the first choice unlocks the whole chain
two disjoint pairs, each pair mutually detonates but the pairs never touch

stress.description: random bomb positions and radii, sized to keep the O(n^3) transitive-closure oracle fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 60))
    bombs = [[rng.randint(1, 200), rng.randint(1, 200), rng.randint(1, 150)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [bombs]

complexity.time: O(n^2) to build the graph, O(n^2) total across all traversals
